rulings.Case Title: MARGARET K REYES VS. RICHARD CURCI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 1, RESPONDENT RICHARD CURCI Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court offers a cautionary note to Petitioner Margaret K. Reyes: The rules of civil procedure must apply equally to parties represented by counsel and those who forgo attorney representation. (Rappleyea v. Campbell, 8 Cal. 4th 975, 984-85 (1994); see Robert J. v. Catherine D., 171 Cal. App. 4th 1500, 1527 (2009) [holding "pro per litigants are held to the same standard as parties represented by trained legal counsel"]. Respondent's Motion to Compel Further Discovery Responses is GRANTED. Petitioner is ORDERED to serve complete, responsive, straightforward and verified responses to Respondent's Special Interrogatories 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17, 18, and 19 within 10 days of service of the Notice of Entry of Order on this motion. Petitioner is ORDERED to serve complete, responsive, straightforward and verified responses compliant with CCP § 2033.220(a) to Respondent's Requests for Admissions 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 21, 23, 24, 26, 27, 28 and 29 within 10 days of service of the Notice of Entry of Order on this motion. Petitioner is ORDERED to serve complete, responsive, straightforward and verified responses to Respondent's Form Interrogatories 2.5, 12.6, 17.1. Petitioner's evasive, irrelevant and nonsensical responses were made without substantial justification, represent an abuse of the discovery process and imposition of monetary sanctions is appropriate under CCP 2023.030(a) in the amount of $1,250.00 to be paid by Petitioner to Bertrand, Fox, et al on or before May 15, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/EPS)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: CENTER FOR GENETICS AND SOCIETY ET AL VS. XAVIER BECERRA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 1, RESPONDENT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE, XAVIER BACERRA's DEMURRER TO AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION. Judge Schulman discloses that he is professionally acquainted with Plaintiffs' counsel Michael T. Risher and Stephanie Lacambra, which will not affect his ability to fairly and impartially decide this matter. Hearing required at 1:30 p.m. At the hearing, counsel should be prepared to address the following questions: 1. Does the Attorney General contest that Plaintiffs have standing to bring this action under the doctrine of public-interest mandamus standing and/or taxpayer standing under Code Civ. Proc. § 526a? 2. Because the named Plaintiffs do not allege that they (or persons they directly represent) were arrested or required to provide DNA samples, does the petition seek to raise a facial challenge to the DNA Act? What is the practical consequence, if any, of characterizing the petition as a facial or as-applied challenge? 3. Are any of Plaintiffs' claims foreclosed by the California Supreme Court's decision in People v. Buza (2018) 4 Cal.5th 658? 4. In the privacy analysis, does the retention of DNA samples represent a significant intrusion on a privacy interest, despite the restrictions placed on the use of DNA samples retained by the Government? (See Pen. Code § 299.5; People v. Buza, 4 Cal.5th at 666-667.) Do the State's interests in analyzing DNA samples for "cold hits" on unsolved crimes or in exonerating suspects of crimes constitute substantial countervailing interests that are furthered by its retention of arrestees' DNA samples? Are there feasible and effective alternatives to retaining DNA samples that could further the same interests with a lesser impact on privacy interests? 5. Is the fourth cause of action (unlawful use of taxpayer funds, Code Civ. Proc. § 526a) independent of the other causes of action? 6. In the event the Court sustains the demurrer in whole or in part, do Plaintiffs seek leave to amend the petition and, if so, in what respects? =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT RAINBOW WATERPROOFING & RESTORATION CO.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. Continued on the Court's own motion to June 25, 2019. Defendant and Cross-Defendant Rainbow Waterproofing & Restoration Co. filed its motion for summary judgment or in the alternative for summary adjudication on February 15, 2019. On April 25, it filed a motion for a good faith determination as to its settlement with Plaintiff, which is currently set for hearing on June 5, 2019. According to counsel to the Developer Defendants and Patrick McNerney, Rainbow refused their requests to withdraw its summary judgment motion or to continue the hearing date on that motion pending the Court's decision on the good faith settlement motion so as to allow them to avoid preparing and filing an opposition to the motion that might be unnecessary or mooted in part. On April 29, however, Rainbow then filed an ex parte application seeking to continue the hearing on the summary judgment motion. However, its counsel failed to appear on the Court's ex parte calendar on April 30, the day before the scheduled hearing on the motion. The hearing is continued on the Court's own motion because the disposition of the summary judgment motion necessarily will depend in part on the Court's ruling on the good faith settlement motion. However, the Court admonishes Rainbow that its conduct has caused both counsel and the Court unnecessary burden and expense. The Court reserves the right to award sanctions against Rainbow and/or its counsel for such conduct; any motion for such relief may be heard concurrently with either of the pending motions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for cross-complainants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH WRIGHT VS. AMANDA NOWINSKI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted And For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant And Cross-Complainant Defendant Amanda Nowinski And Her Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT WAHIB TELDJA, C&J LEASING, INC. DOING BUSINESS AS ROYAL TAXI's Motion To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted And For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant And Cross-Complainant Defendant Amanda Nowinski And Her Counsel. Cross-Complainant and Defendant Wahib Bou Teldja's motion to have requests for admission deemed admitted is denied. (Code Civ. Proc. § 2033.280(c); St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 762, 776 ["a responding party's service, prior to the hearing on the 'deemed admitted' motion, of substantially compliant responses, will defeat a propounding party's attempt under section 2033.280 to have the RFAs deemed admitted."].) The request for sanctions is denied. Although it is normally mandatory that the court impose a monetary sanction on the party or attorney, or both, whose failure to serve a timely response to requests for admission necessitated a motion to compel (id.), the Court declines to do so under the unusual circumstances presented here. Defendant Amanda Nowinski's counsel declares he never received the requests for admissions. (Purtell Decl. 2.) Further, Teldja filed his motion without first meeting and conferring with opposing counsel, and Nowinski served verified responses to the requests for admissions within three days after the motion was filed, which suggests that the need to file the motion could have been avoided entirely, had counsel communicated in advance. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BATROA PROFIT SHARING PLAN (BATROA) ET AL VS. DIMITRIOU &ASSOCIATES, P.C. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Contest And Object To Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT DIMITRIOU & ASSOC. P.C., ANDREW DIMITRIOU's Motion To Contest And Object To Good Faith Settlement. Defendants Dimitriou & Associates, PC and Andrew Dimitriou's motion to contest good faith settlement is denied. The settling defendants provide substantial evidence regarding the nature and extent of their liability and Mr. Dimitriou fails to show that the settlement is out of the ballpark. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(d) [the party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue]; Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499; Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1350, n. 6; Toyota Motor Sales USA, Inc. v. Superior Court (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 864, 871;) The settlors have agreed that they will pay the remaining balance of their policy limits, which they estimate to be approximately $250,000. (Carlson Decl. 3-5.) Further, their declarations show that their other assets are modest. (Jones Decl. 3, 6-13; Jones Decl. (on behalf of Jones & Devoy LLP) 2-6; Cebrian Decl. 4-8.) (See Dole Food Co., Inc. v. Superior Court (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 894, 909 [relevant considerations include the financial conditions and insurance policy limits of settling defendants].) In the Contra Costa action that is the basis for this malpractice case, plaintiff sought to recover its $800,000 investment. Even if the Court approximates Plaintiff's approximate total recovery at trial as $1,000,000, the settlement is in the ballpark-that is, within the reasonable range of the settling defendants' proportional share of comparative liability for the plaintiff's injuries. (Hellam v. Crane Co. (2015) 239 Cal.App.4th 851, 864.) While the OSC that caused the dismissal was sent to Mr. Cebrian, it was addressed to him at Mr. Dimitriou's office. (RJN, Ex. 1 at 43, 46.) Mr. Dimitriou caused the confusion in plaintiff's representation by filing a "notice of disassociation," but never truly withdrawing from the representation. (RJN, Ex. 1 at 28; Helbraun Decl., Ex. 5 (Levine's response to special interrogatory 2.) While Mr. Cebrian made special appearances for plaintiffs, he never became the attorney of record and forwarded all notices to Mr. Dimitriou. (Helbraun Decl., Ex. 6 (Cebrian's response to special interrogatory 43.) Further, Mr. Dimitriou was counsel of record on appeal but failed to file a timely opening brief, causing the appeal from the denial of the motion to vacate to be dismissed. Given this record, the Court finds that Mr. Dimitriou fails to show that the settlement is "out of the ballpark." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION VS. BULL OUTDOOR PRODUCTS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant The Allen Company, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant The Allen Company, Inc. Plaintiff Ecological Rights Foundation's motion to approve Proposition 65 settlement and for entry of consent judgment as to Defendant The Allen Company, Inc. is granted. No opposition filed, and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the warning that is required by the settlement complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount (which is $1,000, not $5,000 as mistakenly indicated on page 9 of the memorandum) is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2). rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT, MARCO BIANCH's Motion To Strike Portions Of First Amended Complaint. Defendant Marco Bianchi's motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is denied. The allegations that Mr. Bianchi aided with the alleged tormenting of Plaintiff are sufficient to support the claim. A reasonable trier of fact could find that Mr. Bianchi's participation in the wrongful conduct-dangling a child over a second-floor balcony and pretending to drop him, putting him in fear for his life-is malicious or despicable conduct. (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1212 [punitive damages are typically awarded for intentional torts including false imprisonment and intentional infliction of emotional distress].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT, MARCO BIANCHI's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant San Francisco Unified School District and Marco Bianchi's demurrer to Plaintiff's First Amended Complaint is sustained with leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10(f).) The amended complaint is uncertain as Plaintiff fails to comply with California Rule of Court 2.112 and separately state his distinct theories of recovery. Plaintiff is given 20 days leave to amend to set forth separately each count or cause of action in compliance with the Rule of Court. For example, a claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress shall be alleged separately from a claim for false imprisonment. Paragraph 20 of the complaint alleges sufficient facts for vicarious/supervisory liability. Whether the District was on sufficient notice regarding the individual defendants' propensity to engage in the misconduct is a factual issue that discovery will resolve. Plaintiff has leave to amend to clarify that he is pursuing a claim pursuant to Gov't Code § 815.2(a) ["A public entity is liable for injury proximately caused by an act or omission of an employee of the public entity within the scope of his employment if the act or omission would, apart from this section, have given rise to a cause of action against that employee or his personal representative."].) (See C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 865-866 ["Ample case authority establishes that school personnel owe students under their supervision a protective duty of ordinary care, for breach of which the school district may be held vicariously liable. [Citations.] If a supervisory or administrative employee of the school district is proven to have breached that duty by negligently exposing plaintiff to a foreseeable danger . . . , resulting in his injuries, and assuming no immunity provision applies, liability falls on the school district under section 815.2."].) It appears that Plaintiff concedes that he cannot pursue an action based on Education Code §§ 49000 and 49001 and therefore, there appears no reason why those statutes should be referenced in the Second Amended Complaint. (See Clausing v. San Francisco Unified School Dist. (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 1224, 1239 ["In short, the provisions of Government Code section 815.6 are not applicable to Education Code sections 49000 and 49001."] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ADI SIMAYOF VS. JODY TAYLOR ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Motion For Attorney_S Fees And Costs Pursuant To C.C.P. 425.16
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT JODY TAYLOR, JASON KORT, GREGORY O'CONNELL, JEREMY JESSUP's Motion For Attorney's Fees And Costs Pursuant To C.C.P. 425.16. Defendants Jody Taylor, Jeremy Jessup, Jason Kort, and Gregory O'Connell's motion for an award of attorney's fees and costs pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 425.16 is granted in the amount of $36,147.50 in attorney's fees and $2,542.43 in costs. Because Defendants prevailed on their special motion to strike the complaint, they are entitled to recover their attorney's fees and costs. (Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(c)(1).) However, the statute's legislative history shows that "the Legislature intended that a prevailing defendant on a motion to strike be allowed to recover attorney fees and costs only on the motion to strike, not the entire suit." (Lafayette Morehouse, Inc. v. Chronicle Publishing Co. (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 1379, 1383 [holding that trial court erred when it awarded defendants fees for the entire suit]; see also S. B. Beach Properties v. Berti (2006) 39 Cal.4th 374, 381 ["the fee 'provision applies only to the motio to strike and not to the entire action.'"]; Wainland v. Law Offices of Mastagni, Holstedt & Chuiurazzi (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 15, 21-22 [assuming section 425.16(c) is limited to an award of costs and attorney fees incurred in connection with the motion to strike, which encompasses costs and attorney fees incurred in challenging the undertaking submitted by the plaintiff to stay enforcement of the award pending appeal].) In calculating the award, therefore, the Court has excluded all but 3.7 hours of Mr. Gramly's time and has excluded 4.0 hours of Ms. Levy's time, as the excluded time was spent in connection with a demurrer, motion to strike, discovery, and other matters rather than in connection with the anti-SLAPP motion. Defendants have made a prima facie showing that the hourly rates they seek are reasonable, and Plaintiff has not presented any contrary evidence. Finally, Defendants are entitled to recover costs as prevailing parties, without regard to whether those costs were incurred in connection with the anti-SLAPP motion. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 1032(a)(4), 1033.5.) Moreover, Plaintiff failed to file a timely motion to strike or tax costs, as required by the California Rules of Court, and therefore is barred from challenging the amount of costs sought by Defendants. (Cal. R. Ct. 3.1700(b)(1), (4).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. BUDGET PREPAY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Daniel R. Hyde, Iii To Quash Service Of Summons Based On Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT DANIEL HYDE's Motion Of Daniel R. Hyde, Iii To Quash Service Of Summons Based On Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction. Defendant Daniel R. Hyde, III's motion to quash is continued to August 19, 2019 to allow for jurisdictional discovery. It is Plaintiff's burden to establish jurisdiction and it must present evidence to implicate the alter ego doctrine. (See Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 539-540.) Here, the Poschl declaration does not show that Mr. Hyde has sufficient minimum contacts with California or is the alter ego of Budget Prepay, Inc. such that the Court may exercise jurisdiction over him. This action is solely for breach of contract and Plaintiff's cases that provide that a corporate officer can be liable for his/her own torts and it is proper to exercise jurisdiction over that officer are inapposite. (See Wolf Designs, Inc. v. DHR Co. (C.D. Cal. 2004) 322 F.Supp.2d 1065, 1072 [" 'Under the fiduciary shield doctrine a person's mere association with a corporation that causes injury in the forum state is not sufficient in itself to permit that forum to assert jurisdiction over the person.'"].) Plaintiff can seek to depose Mr. Hyde and serve 20 special interrogatories and 10 requests for production on him, with such discovery limited to the issue of personal jurisdiction and Plaintiff's alter ego claim. (See Burdick v. Superior Court (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 8, 30.) The supplemental opposition is due on July 29, 2019 and the supplemental reply is due on August 9, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARK WEATHERS VS. YELP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Civil Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT YELP INC.'s Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Civil Proceedings.(part 1 of 2) Defendant Yelp Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration is granted and the action is stayed pending the arbitration of plaintiff Mark Weathers' claims. It is undisputed that the parties entered into an arbitration agreement that covers all the claims Weathers has stated in his complaint. The agreement is enforceable because Mr. Weathers has not shown that it has any indicia of procedural unconscionability. (See Tiri v. Lucky Chances, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 231, 239 ["arbitration agreements are valid and enforceable, unless they are revocable for reasons under state law that would render any contract revocable. Reasons that would render any contract revocable under state law include fraud, duress, and unconscionability."]) An agreement to arbitrate is unconscionable only if both procedural and substantive unconscionability are present. (See Performance Team Freight Systems, Inc. v. Aleman (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 1233, 1247 ["Substantive unconscionability alone does not render a contract unenforceable, however. Procedural unconscionability must also be shown."]; Sanchez v. Carmax Auto Superstores California, LLC (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 398, 402.) Weathers has not submitted any argument nor has he presented any evidence to support his burden to show procedural unconscionability. (See Performance Team Freight Systems, Inc., 241 Cal.App.4th at 1248 ["Absent any evidence, we cannot just assume there was procedural unconscionability."].) The arbitration agreement was not signed by Weathers as a condition of his employment because it provided for a meaningful opportunity to opt out of the Dispute Resolution Policy. (See Davis v. O'Melveny & Myers (9th Cir. 2007) 485 F.3d 1066, 1073 ["if an employee has a meaningful opportunity to opt out of the arbitration provision when signing the agreement and still preserve his or her job, then it is not procedurally unconscionable."]; see also Securitas Security Services USA, Inc. v. Superior Court (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 1109, 1123 ["The fact Edwards was given an opportunity to opt out of the agreement also may be an indication that the dispute resolution agreement was not an adhesion contract; that it was free from procedural unconscionability."].) The Dispute Resolution Policy provides that the employee has thirty days from the day the Policy is made available to him to complete and send an opt out form to the Human Resources Department, whose physical and email address is given. (Louridge Decl. Exh.D, §9.) Weathers was given notice of the existence of the opt out provision in the Offer Letter as well. (Louridge Decl. Exh.B). Furthermore, there are no other indicia of procedural unconscionability: the agreement was a standalone document; Weathers was made aware of its existence in the Handbook by signing the Offer Letter, and viewing the e-signature receipt; there is no evidence that Weathers did not understand the terms of the agreement or his right to opt out. (See Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Ahmed (9th Cir. 2002) 283 F.3d 1198, 1200). (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARK WEATHERS VS. YELP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Yelp Inc. Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Civil Proceedings (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 1, 2019, Line 13, Defendant Yelp Inc.'s Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Civil Proceedings (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling) Because the arbitration agreement is not procedurally unconscionable, the Court need not reach Weathers' arguments regarding substantive unconscionability. In any event, his principal argument-that it would be unconscionable to require him to pay fees and costs, if his claim is determined to be frivolous-is meritless. The agreement specifically provides that "in all cases where required by law, Yelp will pay the arbitrator's and arbitration fees." That provision is not ambiguous, nor does it contravene Armendariz v. Foundational Health Psychcare Servs., Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83. Such costs are not unique to arbitration because they could be equally imposed by a court. (See Williams v. Chino Valley Independent Fire Dist. (2015) 61 Cal.4th 97, 99-100.) The parties shall promptly commence arbitration. The Court declines Plaintiff's request to appoint an arbitrator. The parties have already agreed to a specific arbitrator. (McConathy Decl. 10-11 & Exs. K, L.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Yelp is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)
